exemplar.title: How has climate change and the rise of global temperatures affected the number of wildfires across Australia.

detailedExemplarData.title: How has climate change and the rise of global temperatures affected the number of wildfires across Australia.

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Consistent use of key environmental terminology
Report follows IA structure with appropriate headings
weaknesses: Verbose phrasing and occasional imprecise language disrupt concision
Logical flow is interrupted by missing transitions and formatting issues
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and use of terminology meet band 2 descriptors, but inconsistent formatting, incomplete references and flow breaks prevent a higher score, yielding 2 marks.

strengths: Clearly stated, coherent research question focused on wildfire extent and temperature rise
Rich contextualization using the Black Summer case study with relevant statistics
Logical explanation of how temperature influences wildfire risk
weaknesses: Terminology around ânumber of wildfiresâ needs precision (e.g., hectares burned) to sharpen focus
Control of confounding factors (e.g., rainfall variability) is not fully explained
Some citations lack full reference details, weakening credibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall performance sits at the top of band 3â4. The research question is focused and relevant, and the environmental context (Black Summer data, consequences) is well developed. Minor omissions in term definition and source detail prevent reaching band 5â6, but the strength of contextual links justifies a score of 5.

strengths: Method outline is repeatable at a basic level (secondary data + correlation test)
weaknesses: No sampling strategy for spatial or temporal data selection
Risk assessment and ethical considerations are entirely absent
Insufficient detail on data-selection criteria and software procedures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methodology is described in simple bullet points and would yield some relevant data, placing it in band 1â2. The complete lack of sampling rationale and ethical considerations limits this criterion to 2 marks.

strengths: Evaluates the weak correlation in environmental context and acknowledges other driving factors
Identifies strengths (recent decade data) and some weaknesses (limited variables) in the method
Proposes further research directions addressing key aspects of fire management
weaknesses: Evaluation is descriptive rather than fully analytical (no evidence-strength weighting)
Improvements focus more on policy than refining the investigative method
Suggestions for extensions are somewhat superficial
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance falls solidly in band 3â4: the conclusion is evaluated against environmental implications, with clear method critique. Omissions in systematic limitations analysis and only superficial extensions prevent a higher score, warranting a 4.

strengths: All relevant data are presented in clear, labeled graphs with uncertainty margins
Appropriate use of trend lines and regression equations
Acknowledgement of data variability through uncertainty ranges
weaknesses: Statistical analysis omits p-values or confidence intervals
Inconsistent axis labeling and scaling in places
Anomalies are described qualitatively rather than analyzed quantitatively
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation is thorough and accurate, meriting the top of band 3â4. While analysis and conclusions lack full depth (no formal significance tests), the high quality of graphical presentation and correct pattern identification justifies a score of 5.

strengths: Proposes a relevant application (mitigation and adaptation strategies) linked to findings
weaknesses: Justification of the application is general and lacks quantitative support
Evaluation of strengths/weaknesses of the proposed solution is minimal
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application is described with a basic link to results (band 2). However, the evaluation of feasibility and limitations is underdeveloped, restricting this criterion to 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Environmental terminology (e.g., âfeedback loopâ, âhectares burnedâ) is correctly employed, but verbose phrasing and occasional imprecise language (âdrasticallyâ, âalternating rainfallâ) reduce concision.  Either criterion is partially satisfied, placing the work in band 2.

The student evaluates how the weak correlation tempers the broader claim that climate change drives fires, and acknowledges other variables.  However, the evaluation is descriptive, not systematically weighing evidence strength or policy relevance.  This fulfils the 3â4 descriptor but does not reach the comprehensive level of band 5â6.

The conclusion properly references the calculated correlation and recognises its weakness while discussing contributing factors.  Support from presented data is evident, yet the reasoning lacks detailed linkage to statistical outputs (e.g., confidence intervals).  Therefore it meets the descriptors for a valid but only partially developed conclusionâband 3â4, score 3.

Basic statistical analysis (linear regression equations, correlation coefficients) is correct; patterns such as the weak positive correlation are identified.  Yet the analysis is incomplete: uncertainties are ignored, no significance test is applied and anomalies are not quantitatively investigated.  Consequently the strand sits in the 3â4 band but at the lower end (3).

Suggested improvements focus on national fire-management policies rather than refining the investigative method; additional research variables are only vaguely mentioned.  These proposals are superficial, so the strand earns the minimum score in the 1â2 band.

Methodology is limited to three short bullet points describing secondary-data collection and a correlation test.  Key procedural details (data selection criteria, statistical software settings, handling of uncertainties) are absent, yet the outline is repeatable at a basic level and could yield some relevant data.  Because it is only partially appropriate and clearly insufficient for a robust investigation, the low end of the 1â2 band is chosen, awarding 2 marks.

Links between the environmental issue and the specific research focus are described: rising temperatures â drier vegetation â higher wildfire risk; the narrative also explains anomalies such as Black Summer through additional climatic factors.  However, the explanation is partial, lacking a systematic treatment of all variables and how the chosen correlation test isolates temperature from confounding factors.  Some omissions keep the strand in the 3â4 bracket, with a score of 3 reflecting those gaps.

The research question is clearly stated, focused on a single dependent variable (number/extent of wildfires) and a single driving factor (rise in global temperatures linked to climate change).  The phrasing is coherent and unambiguous, allowing a reader to understand exactly what the investigation intends to test.  Although terminology could be tightened ("number" is later treated as âhectares burnedâ), the intent remains sufficiently precise to merit the upper region of the 3â4 band rather than the highest band that demands consistent sharp focus.

The evaluation of the proposed solution is limited to asserting that measures would reduce risk; specific strengths (e.g., feasibility, cost) or weaknesses (e.g., implementation barriers) are not examined.  This meets the minimal descriptor of band 1.

The report follows the prescribed IA sections and uses headings, but coherence is disrupted by repeated citation fragments and inconsistent formatting.  Structure is therefore present yet not sustained throughout, aligning with band 2.

Some strengths (use of recent decade of data) and weaknesses (limited variables) are briefly noted, but limitations such as small sample size, secondary-data reliability and lack of controls are not analysed.  The strand therefore remains in the 1â2 band, with 2 marks for identifying more than one aspect.

The student outlines the key environmental issueâAustraliaâs increasing wildfire activity in the context of climate changeâby giving recent statistics (e.g., 17 million ha burned during Black Summer) and mentioning ecological, health and feedback-loop consequences.  While this provides relevant context, the discussion remains largely descriptive and cites only a handful of generic secondary sources; the analysis does not yet reach the depth of a sustained âdiscussionâ required for the top band.  Hence mid-band (3) is appropriate.

No sampling strategy (spatial, temporal or data-set selection) is mentioned or justified.  The work relies on convenience secondary data with no explanation of representativeness, so the strand does not reach the minimum descriptor for band 1â2.

Risk assessment and ethical considerations are completely absent; the student neither lists basic risks (e.g., data-use ethics, site access) nor addresses publication ethics.  The strand therefore remains at 0.

All collected data are presented graphically (three scatter/line graphs) with axes, units and trend lines.  Minor shortcomings include inconsistent axis labels and poorly scaled y-axes, but overall the diagrams are appropriate and include every data set referenced.  This aligns with the upper portion of band 3â4.

One applicationâdeveloping mitigation and adaptation strategiesâis described with a link to findings (temperature rise increases risk).  Justification is general and lacks quantitative backing, matching the descriptor for band 2 (described with weak justification).

The narrative generally follows a logical sequence from background to conclusion; nevertheless, jumps between paragraphs, missing transitions and citation placeholders interrupt flow, making some sections difficult to follow.  This satisfies âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, i.e., band 2.

annotations.comment: Reporting only the correlation coefficient (0.2331) without interpreting its strength may mislead readers; discuss whether this value is statistically meaningful.
The second correlation coefficient (0.1721) differs from earlier 0.2331. Resolve this inconsistency to maintain coherent data analysis.
Analysis narrative restates background rather than examining anomalies quantitatively. Integrate statistical anomaly analysis to deepen interpretation.
The research question clearly identifies variables but ânumber of wildfiresâ could be defined more precisely (e.g., hectares burned) to sharpen focus.
The hypothesis predicts both frequency and intensity, yet only area burned is measured. Aligning hypothesis variables with measurable data will improve testability.
Background provides key Black Summer statistics, anchoring the environmental issue in a real event. This strengthens contextual understanding.
The statement on future climate model predictions lacks citation details. Adding full references will bolster credibility and context.
The narrative links temperature rise to drought and fire risk but omits how confounding factors will be controlled in analysis. Consider clarifying this connection.
Method steps are outlined but lack detail on data selection criteria and software settings. Expanding these details will enhance repeatability.
No sampling strategy is described for spatial or temporal data selection. Justify why chosen years or regions represent the broader Australian context.
The list of temperature source links is unclear and inconsistent. Use a standard citation format to improve communication of data provenance.
Temperature table presents raw values but percent symbols may confuse readers. Clarify units (e.g., Â°C anomaly) and consider error margins.
Graph caption lacks details on axes scaling and data sources. Adding a concise caption with axis labels will improve clarity of presentation.
Using a hand-drawn regression tool is appropriate for basic analysis, but specifying software and significance tests would strengthen methodological rigor.
Uncertainty margins in the fire-area table are a strength, as they acknowledge data variability and support more nuanced analysis.
Values in the burned-area table use inconsistent significant figures. Standardizing to two significant figures will improve readability.
The scatter plot effectively shows a trend but omits a p-value or confidence interval. Including these would substantiate the correlationâs significance.
Paragraph repeats known mechanisms without linking back to quantitative findings. Focus on integrating data trends with environmental processes.
Discussion acknowledges weak correlation and other factors but lacks evaluation of evidence strength. Consider comparing effect sizes or policy relevance.
The cited susceptibility of dry crops lacks linkage to measured data; propose how to quantify this factor in future investigations.
The Black Summer case study is a strong example illustrating the anomaly; it enriches the investigationâs environmental context.
The Black Summer narrative lacks an in-text citation and formal reference entry. Add a full source to validate the description.
Conclusion summarizes findings but does not quantify limitations. Adding confidence intervals or effect sizes would deepen conclusion validity.
Applications are mentioned generically; justify proposed mitigation or adaptation strategies with data-driven rationale.
Bibliography header lacks consistent style and numbering. Applying a uniform referencing format (e.g., APA) will improve communication.
References are incomplete (missing authors, dates). Ensure each entry includes full bibliographic details to meet academic standards.
